name: Env

aliases.name: Env
aliases.comment:
Pack together the current value along with a computed view of said value.

We can specialize the `a` to be `Html a`
and get something that works for top level programs.

We can also not do that.
We can describe many UIs and they don't have to be `Html a`.

values.name: duplicate
values.comment:
Create a new env where the view is another env.

Gives us the lazy unfolding of all future states of a given env.
values.type: Env e a -> Env e (Env e a)

values.name: extract
values.comment: View the current value.
values.type: Env e a -> a

values.name: map
values.comment:
Transform the view.

Useful when you want to change the view of an already defined env.
values.type: (a -> b) -> Env e a -> Env e b

values.name: move
values.comment:
Move to the new given value.

This is how we make progress in the UI.
values.type: e -> Env e a -> Env e a

values.name: set
values.comment:
Replace the view.

A common pattern is to just replace the old view without depending on it:
`set x = map (\_ -> x)`
values.type: b -> Env s a -> Env s b

values.name: toBeginnerProgram
values.comment:
Convert to a "beginner program" for use in a `main` function.

    import Env exposing (Env)
    import Html exposing (Html)
    import Html.Events
    import String.Extra

    main : Program Never (Env Int (Int -> Html Int)) Int
    main =
        Html.beginnerProgram
            (Env.toBeginnerProgram { here = 0, view = view })

    view : Int -> Html Int
    view n =
        Html.div
            []
            [ Html.button
                [ Html.Events.onClick (n - 1) ]
                [ Html.text "-" ]
            , Html.div
                []
                [ Html.text (String.Extra.fromInt n) ]
            , Html.button
                [ Html.Events.onClick (n + 1) ]
                [ Html.text "+" ]
            ]

View this example on Ellie: <https://ellie-app.com/fLBqHbdNLa1/0>
values.type: Env e (e -> Html a) -> { model : Env e (e -> Html a), update : e -> Env e (e -> Html a) -> Env e (e -> Html a), view : Env e (e -> Html a) -> Html a }

name: Traced

unions.name: Traced
unions.comment:
Pack together an accumulator, a unique value,
and a function to view the value.

Accumulates new values together in most operations.

This is very similar to `Store a b`.
The difference being that a `Traced a b` accumulates the `a`
as it unfolds all future states,
whereas a `Store a b` replaces all `a`s as it unfolds all future states.

values.name: addition
values.comment:
Creates a traced that accumulates with addition.

The unique value is 0.
values.type: Traced number number

values.name: duplicate
values.comment:
Create a new traced where the view is another traced.

Gives us the lazy unfolding of all future states of a given traced.
values.type: Traced m a -> Traced m (Traced m a)

values.name: extract
values.comment: View the current value.
values.type: Traced m a -> a

values.name: list
values.comment:
Creates a traced that accumulates lists.

The unique value is the empty list.
values.type: Traced (List a) (List a)

values.name: map
values.comment:
Transform the view.

Useful when you want to change the view of an already defined traced.
values.type: (a -> b) -> Traced m a -> Traced m b

values.name: move
values.comment:
Move to the new given value.

This is how we make progress in the UI.
values.type: m -> Traced m a -> Traced m a

values.name: multiplication
values.comment:
Creates a traced that accumulates with multiplication.

The unique value is 1.
values.type: Traced number number

values.name: set
values.comment:
Replace the view.

A common pattern is to just replace the old view without depending on it:
`set x = map (\_ -> x)`
values.type: b -> Traced m a -> Traced m b

values.name: string
values.comment:
Creates a traced that accumulates strings.

The unique value is the empty string.
values.type: Traced String String

values.name: toBeginnerProgram
values.comment:
Convert to a "beginner program" for use in a `main` function

    import Html exposing (Html)
    import Html.Events
    import String.Extra
    import Traced exposing (Traced)

    main : Program Never (Traced Int (Html Int)) Int
    main =
        Html.beginnerProgram
            (Traced.toBeginnerProgram (Traced.map view Traced.addition))

    view : Int -> Html Int
    view n =
        Html.div
            []
            [ Html.button
                [ Html.Events.onClick -1 ]
                [ Html.text "-" ]
            , Html.div
                []
                [ Html.text (String.Extra.fromInt n) ]
            , Html.button
                [ Html.Events.onClick 1 ]
                [ Html.text "+" ]
            ]

View this example on Ellie: <https://ellie-app.com/Mx3LhD8Fa1/0>
values.type: Traced m (Html a) -> { model : Traced m (Html a), update : m -> Traced m (Html a) -> Traced m (Html a), view : Traced m (Html a) -> Html a }

values.name: view
values.comment:
Returns the view of a traced.

Useful when you want to view the value,
with something other than the unique value of the traced.
values.type: Traced m a -> m -> a

aliases.name: Program
aliases.comment:
An alternative representation of a program.

If we generate a new `model` in both Commands and Subscriptions,
it turns out that we don't need to write an `update` function at all.

This representation will also push us to write a more declarative program
by expressing the model as distinct states.
Rather than thinking, "when we get this message, create this command;"
we start to think, "all models of this shape, produce this command."
aliases.type: { commands : model -> Cmd model, model : model, subscriptions : model -> Sub model }

values.name: program
values.comment: Create a headless program from an alternative program.
values.type: { commands : model -> Cmd model, model : model, subscriptions : model -> Sub model } -> Program Never model model

name: Store

aliases.name: Store
aliases.comment:
Pack together the current value along with a function to view said value.

We can specialize the `a` to be `Html a`
and get something that works for top level programs.

We can also not do that.
We can describe many UIs and they don't have to be `Html a`.

values.name: duplicate
values.comment:
Create a new store where the view is another store.

Gives us the lazy unfolding of all future states of a given store.
values.type: Store s a -> Store s (Store s a)

values.name: extract
values.comment: View the current value.
values.type: Store s a -> a

values.name: map
values.comment:
Transform the view.

Useful when you want to change the view of an already defined store.
values.type: (a -> b) -> Store s a -> Store s b

values.name: move
values.comment:
Move to the new given value.

This is how we make progress in the UI.
values.type: s -> Store s a -> Store s a

values.name: set
values.comment:
Replace the view.

A common pattern is to just replace the old view without depending on it:
`set x = map (\_ -> x)`
values.type: b -> Store m a -> Store m b

values.name: toBeginnerProgram
values.comment:
Convert to a "beginner program" for use in a `main` function.

    import Html exposing (Html)
    import Html.Events
    import Store exposing (Store)
    import String.Extra

    main : Program Never (Store Int (Html Int)) Int
    main =
        Html.beginnerProgram
            (Store.toBeginnerProgram { here = 0, view = view })

    view : Int -> Html Int
    view n =
        Html.div
            []
            [ Html.button
                [ Html.Events.onClick (n - 1) ]
                [ Html.text "-" ]
            , Html.div
                []
                [ Html.text (String.Extra.fromInt n) ]
            , Html.button
                [ Html.Events.onClick (n + 1) ]
                [ Html.text "+" ]
            ]

View this example on Ellie: <https://ellie-app.com/cDGbmY8Ca1/0>
values.type: Store s (Html a) -> { model : Store s (Html a), update : s -> Store s (Html a) -> Store s (Html a), view : Store s (Html a) -> Html a }

name: Html.Alternative

aliases.name: BeginnerProgram
aliases.comment:
An alternative representation of a "beginner" program.

If we generate a new `model` in the HTML,
it turns out that we don't need to write an `update` function at all.

This representation will push us to write a more declarative UI
by expressing the model as distinct states.
aliases.type: { model : model, view : model -> Html model }

aliases.name: Program
aliases.comment:
An alternative representation of a program.

We can extend the idea of a `BeginnerProgram model`
to other contexts like `Sub` and `Cmd`.

If we again generate a new `model` in all of these contexts:
Commands, HTML, and Subscriptions;
it turns out that we don't need to write an `update` function here either.

This representation will also push us to write a more declarative UI
by expressing the model as distinct states.
Rather than thinking, "when we get this message, create this command;"
we start to think, "all models of this shape, produce this command."
aliases.type: { commands : model -> Cmd model, model : model, subscriptions : model -> Sub model, view : model -> Html model }

values.name: beginnerProgram
values.comment: Create a "beginner" program from an alternative program.
values.type: BeginnerProgram model -> Program Never model model

values.name: program
values.comment: Create a program from an alternative program.
values.type: Program model -> Program Never model model

values.name: tuple2
values.comment:
Combine two programs together so both are displayed at once.

This lets us think about programs separately,
yet still combine them declaratively.

If you've read the paper,
this is the Day convolution of two programs with a pre-determined convolution.

If you haven't read the paper,
congratulations, you're using Day convolution without having to understand it!
values.type: Program a -> Program b -> Program ( a, b )
